m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following C program ?
#include<stdio.h>
int main ()
{
    int n = 5;
    switch (n)
    {
        default:
          printf ("default");
          n = 2;
        case 1:
          printf ("1");
        case 2:
          printf ("2");
        case 3:
          printf ("3");
          break;
    }
  return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: As the value of n is 5 which is not maching to any case hence default case get executed. there is no break statement after default case hence all remaining cases also get executed and hence output will be default 1 2 3

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    char a = 'A';
    if (a = = 'a' || (a = 'B') || a)
a = a + 32;
    else
a = 'c';
    printf(" % c", a);
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
a=='a' is false a='B' || a i.e. a has been assigned value 'B'
which is true.
so a=a+32=B+32 => 66+32=98
character value of 98 is b.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which operator cannot be used for joining two conditions ?
m_Item1.Explanation: ! is negation operator which is used to negate a condition and it is unary operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the correct usage of conditional operators used in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: The syntax of ternary is Exp1? Exp2 : Exp3.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is false statement?
m_Item1.Explanation: '32768 is false' is false statement  because 32768 is positive value which is true.

m_Item1.QuestionText:

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program?

int main ()
{
  int k, num = 30;
  k = (num > 5 ? (num <= 10 ? 100 : 200) : 500);
  printf ("%d", num);
  return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: here num = 30 and at the end we are printing num hence 30.Nested ternary is just for confusion. Result of ternary is stored in k.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Point out the error,
if any in the program.
#include <stdio.h>

int main()
{
    int P = 10;
    switch (P)
    {
        case 10:
            printf("Case 1");
 
        case 20:
            printf("Case 2");
            break;

        case P:
            printf("Case 2");
            break;
    }

    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Error: No default value is specified
Error: Constant expression required at line case P
Error: There is no break statement in each case.

m_Item1.Explanation:
Case statement should have constant values.
Error as in swich case we can use either integer constant or character constant. case P is not valid case.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is correct statement ?

Options.OptionText: alone if can occur without else
alone else can occur without if
else should be written before if
else is compulsory for every if

m_Item1.Explanation: if can occur without else  but else can not occur without if.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about nested if-else ?

Options.OptionText: we can write if-else only inside if part
we can write if-else only inside else part
we can write if-else in both if & else part
we can write one if-else after another if-else

m_Item1.Explanation: we can write if-else in both if & else part

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which nested if-else can be converted to switch ?

Options.OptionText: every nested if-else can be converted in to switch
having 1 operand common in all conditions & other operand uncommon
having relational operator = = in all conditions

m_Item1.Explanation: for swich case we required one common operand and == relational operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is not required for writing switch ?
m_Item1.Explanation: Default, case and break are used in switch case . continue is used in loop statements.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about default ?

Options.OptionText: default can be written anywhere in switch
default must be last case in switch
default must be first case in switch

m_Item1.Explanation: default can be written anywhere in switch

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is most false about switch?

Options.OptionText: First case may not have break

m_Item1.Explanation: All cases require continue . Continue is used in loop condition not in Switch.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Switch can not handle_______data type
m_Item1.Explanation: float and double can not used in switch. only int and char used in switch.

m_Item1.QuestionText: switch is preferred over nested if-else because

Options.OptionText: It increases complexity of a program
It improves execution speed of a program
It increases readability of a program
It reduces size of compiled code

m_Item1.Explanation: instead of writing multiple if else statements swich increases readability of program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which character is used in switch to separate case from its action part
m_Item1.Explanation: we have to write : after case statement and then write logic.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which condition is correct to check whether ch is a capital letter ?
m_Item1.Explanation: ch>='A' && ch<='Z'  as this condition will check ch> ='A' and ch <='Z'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:

{

    else
printf(“ % d”, n++);
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
(!(!m)&&m)
=(!(!10)&&10)
=(!(!true)&&true)
=(! false && true)
=!false
=true.hence it willprint true block i.e m++(use nd then increment) so 10 will be printed

m_Item1.QuestionText:

    {
      int a=2,b=5,c=1;
      if(a>b)
      if(a>c)  
     printf("*");
        else
      printf("*");
   printf("+");
    }

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
     int a=0,b=1;

     if(++a ==--b)
       printf("%d %d", --a, b++);
     else
     printf("%d %d", a--, ++b);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation: in if condition a is pre incremented and b is pre decremented hence a is 1 and b is 0 . hence else part get executed and in printf statement a is post decremented hence a is printed as 1 and b is pre incremented hence b becomes 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
     int a=3,b=1;

     if( b>a, a>b)
     printf("%d",--b);
     else
     printf("%d", a++);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation: in If statement when there is ', 'seperated values , it will consider only right most value hence a> b is true and --b i.e. 0 will get printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
if(a>b) b=a;
if(c>b) b=c;
printf("%d", b);

What is represented by above code?

Options.OptionText: it prints value of b as it is
it prints minimum of 3 nos. a, b, c
it prints maximum of 3 nos. a, b, c
it prints average of 3 nos. a, b, c

m_Item1.Explanation: it prints maximum of 3 nos. a, b, c as in code we have check the that if a> b we are assigning that to b and if c> b we are assigning c to b   hence b become maximum of a and c.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    char c = 'a';
    lable: switch (c)
    {
        case 'a':
            ++c;
            goto lable;

        case 'b':
            printf(" % c", c++);

        case 'c':
            printf(" % c", c);
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
step 1: case 'a' get executed .
step 2: as go to statement is used the control goes to again switch statement. c becomes 'b' now. hence case 'b' get executed and as no break statement is used case 'c' also get executed hence output is b c.

m_Item1.QuestionText:

    }
}

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    float ch = 2.5;
    switch (ch)
    {
        case 2.5:
            printf(" % f", ch);

        case 2.0:

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    char ch = 'A';
    switch (ch)
    {
        case (ch >= 65 && ch < 90):
            printf("caps");
            continue;

        case (ch >= 97 && ch <= 122):
            printf("small");
            break;

        default:
            printf("default");
            break;
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: We can not use logical operators in switch case statements.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    int i = 10;
    switch (i)
    {
        case 10:
            printf("Ten");

        case 20:
            printf("Twenty");

        default:
            printf("None");
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: As no break statement is written every case get executed after case 10. hence out put is Ten Twenty None .

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following errors would be reported by the compiler on compiling the program given below?
#include <stdio.h>

int main()
{
    int a = 5;
    switch (a)
    {
        case 1:
            printf("First");

        case 2:
            printf("Second");

        case 3 + 2:
            printf("Third");

        case 5:
            printf("Final");
            break;
    }

    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: There is no break statement in each case.
Expression as in case 3 + 2 is not allowed.

m_Item1.Explanation: as 3+5 is 5 and there is already case 5 is present hence Duplicate case. Duplicate cases not allowed in switch.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is false about switch cases ?

Options.OptionText: cases have to be constant expression

m_Item1.Explanation: case values cannot be string as well as fractions. We can have only char , short,int cases.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {  
int a = 0, b = 2;

 if (a++, b--, --b)
     printf(" % d % d", a, b);
 else
     printf(" % d % d", b, a);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation:
in if execution will go from left to right.but only --b will be placed in if block(When you have , seperated values only right most value get executed).
so a++=1,b--=1 and --b=0.
Hence else will get executed,i.e b=0 and a=1 will be printed

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
     int k=1,b=0,c=++b;

          if( k-- >0 )  
     printf("*");
else
       printf("+");
print("%d", k);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation: k-- means use the value first and then decrement so it will use 1 for comparing.1>0 is true so * will be printed and then value of k which is decrement in above step will be printed i.e 0.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about switch cases ?

Options.OptionText: more than 1 cases having same action can be written together separated by comma
more than 1 cases can be joined by using &&, ||
small case & upper case are treated as same case

m_Item1.Explanation: duplicate case values are not allowed it will result in compilation error.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
     int m = 0, n = 5 / 2 + 3 * 6;

if (!m++ && ++m)
    printf(" % d", m++);
else
    printf(" % d", m--);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation:
( !m++ && ++m)
logical && is used so first left part (!m++)will be executed.In this (!m++) ++ is post incremented hence first it use the value of m and then increment.
!0 and value of m is1,and now right part ++m =2.
1 && 2 is true so true block will be executed

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
   int m = 0, n = -1;
  if (~m && ~n)
    printf ("%d", n);
  else
    printf ("+%d", ~m);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation:
~m= -1 and ~n=0 i.e true and false =false
so else part will be executed.

Remember that negative numbers are stored as the two's complement of the positive counterpart. As an example, here's the representation of -2 in two's complement: (8 bits)

1111 1110
The way you get this is by taking the binary representation of a number, taking its complement (inverting all the bits) and adding one. Two starts as 0000 0010, and by inverting the bits we get 1111 1101. Adding one gets us the result above. The first bit is the sign bit, implying a negative.

So let's take a look at how we get ~2 = -3:

Here's two again:

0000 0010
Simply flip all the bits and we get:

1111 1101
Well, what's -3 look like in two's complement? Start with positive 3: 0000 0011, flip all the bits to 1111 1100, and add one to become negative value (-3), 1111 1101.

So if you simply invert the bits in 2, you get the two's complement representation of -3.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
     int  a=2,b=3;

     if(a<=b)
     if(a=b) 
printf("**");;
     else
     printf("****");
    }
m_Item1.Explanation:
step1: a<=b i.e 2<=3 is true so next if statement will be executed.
step 2: a=b i.e 3 means true so inner if true block will be executed.Hence output will be **.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
int m=3, n=4;
  
if(m&n)
  printf("%d", m--);
 else
printf("%d", n++);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation: binary of 3=0011 and binary of 4=0100 & operation on it will give 0000 which is 0 i.e false so false block will be executed and n++ i.e 4 will be printed

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is possible ?

Options.OptionText: all of the above are possible

m_Item1.Explanation: Nesting of switch case, nesting of if else , all possiblities are allowed in C.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    float j = 0.1;
    switch (j)
    {
        case 0.1:
            printf(" % f", j++);
            continue;

        case 0.2:
            printf(" % f", j);
            break;

        default:
            printf("Default");
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: For Swich case only integer,short and charachters  are allowed. Float values are not allowed in Swich case.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
      int n=4;
      switch( n )
       {
        default :
printf("def");
        case  4 :
printf("%d", n);
       }
    }
m_Item1.Explanation: case 4 is matching so it will display the out put as 4.default case is only executed if no matching case is found and it is always executed at the end.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
    char c = 'A';
  switch (c)
    {
    case ('c' == 'A'):
      printf ("caps");

    default:
      printf ("small");
    }
     }
m_Item1.Explanation: Case should be character constant or integer constant. 'c'=='A' results into false which is not matching. hence dfault case get executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    int i = 3;
    switch(i)
    {
        default:
            printf("0");

        case 1:
            printf("l");

        case 2:
            printf("2");
            break;

        case 3:
            printf("3");
            break;
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Default case always executed at the end if no maching case found. hence here i= 3 has matching cse hence that case get executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output if you will compile and execute the following c code?
void main()
{
    int a = 2;
    if (a == 2)
    {
        a = ~a + 2 << 1;

    }
    else
    {
        break;
    }
}

break can be used  only in  loop or in switch. It is invalid in case of if /else statement.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output if you will compile and execute the following c code?

In c while declaring any static variable, you have to assign some constant value, here

What will be output when you will execute following c code?

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    char val = 2;
    int result;
    switch (val)
    {
        default:
            result = 0;

        case '1':
            result = 10;

        case '2':
            result = 20;

        case '3':
            result = 30;
}

    printf(" %d", result);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: value for val is 2 i.e. ascii value 2 & that is not '1' or '2' or '3', so default case will execute & no break in default or in any case so after default all below cases will execute, so result = 30

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#define L 10
void main()
{
    auto money = 10;
    switch (money, money *2)
    {
        case L:
            printf("Willian");
            break;

        case L *2:
            printf("Warren");
            break;

        case L *3:
            printf("Carlos");
            break;

        default:
            printf("Lawrence");

        case L *4:
            printf("Inqvar");
            break;
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
All occurences of L are replaced by 10.
In switch, the last parameter(money*2) is considered.
Therefore, case L*2 matches.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    float me = 1.1; double you = 1.1;
    if (me == you)
        printf("Hello");
    else
        printf("Bye");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: double has 2x more precision then float.Hence when this numbers are stored in binary format in memory they are not same hence me is not equal to you and output is Bye.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    int a = 500, b = 100, c;

    if (!a >= 400)
        b = 300;

        c = 200;
    printf("b = %d c = %d ",  b, c);
}
What will be the output ?
m_Item1.Explanation: !a is 0 hence 0>=400 is false condition hence next statement get executed hence c=200 and b is already defined to 100. hence output will be 100,200.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the correct syntax for an if condition in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C, the correct syntax for an if statement includes parentheses for the condition followed by curly braces for the block of code.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code?
int a = 10;
if (a > 5)
    printf("Hello");
else
    printf("World");
m_Item1.Explanation: Since the condition (a > 5) is true, the code inside the if block executes, printing 'Hello'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code:
int x = 0;
if (x = 5)
    printf("Yes!");
else
    printf("No!");
What will be printed?
m_Item1.Explanation: The assignment operator (=) is used instead of the equality operator (==), which assigns 5 to x, resulting in true (non-zero). So, 'Yes!' is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following types can be used as a condition in an if statement in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: Any non-zero value of these types is interpreted as true, so integer, float, and character can be used in conditions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the result of executing the following code?
int a = 3;
if (a == 3)
    printf("3");
    printf("Three");
m_Item1.Explanation: Only the first printf is controlled by the if statement. 'Three' will always be printed because it is outside the if statement's control.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C, which of the following expressions evaluates to true?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C, any non-zero value is considered true. -1 is non-zero, so it evaluates to true.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the best practice for using an if statement in C?

Options.OptionText: Always include curly braces for both the if and else blocks.
Use a comma instead of a semicolon.
Write the if condition without parentheses.
Avoid using if statements for integer comparisons.

m_Item1.Explanation: Even if the block contains a single statement, it is best practice to use curly braces to avoid potential logic errors.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the following code snippet output?
int x = 5, y = 10;
if (x > y)
    if (x < y)
        printf("A");
    else
        printf("B");
else
    printf("C");
m_Item1.Explanation: The first condition (x > y) is false, so the inner if-else isn't executed. 'C' is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which alternative to using an if statement can be used for better performance if there are many conditions?
m_Item1.Explanation: A switch statement can provide better performance with multiple conditions and is more readable for multiple discrete values.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the code:
if (1)
    printf("True");
else
    printf("False");
What will be printed?
m_Item1.Explanation: Since the condition is 1 (non-zero), it is true, so 'True' is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What error, if any, is present in the following code?
if (x == 10);
    printf("Value is 10");
m_Item1.Explanation: The semicolon after the if condition ends the if statement, causing the subsequent printf to always execute.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider the statement: if (x = 0). What kind of issue could arise from this condition?
m_Item1.Explanation: This is a logical error because assignment is used instead of comparison. It sets x to 0 and evaluates as false.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is correct syntax for an if statement in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C, an if statement is written as if(condition) { statements; }.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of an else block in an if-else statement?

Options.OptionText: It is optional and executes when if condition is true
It is mandatory and executes when if condition is false
It is optional and executes when if condition is false
It can execute instead of the if statement

m_Item1.Explanation: The else block is optional and executes when the if condition evaluates to false.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the correct way to compare two integers, a and b, for equality in an if condition?
m_Item1.Explanation: The correct operator for equality comparison is ==, so the condition should be if (a == b).

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What would be the output of the following code?
int a = 5;
if (a > 3) {
printf("Greater");
} else {
printf("Lesser");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Since a (5) is greater than 3, the condition is true, so it prints "Greater".

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is used to specify multiple conditional branches?
m_Item1.Explanation: The keyword for specifying multiple conditional branches in C is else if.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about nested if-else statements?

Options.OptionText: Nested if-else statements are not allowed in C
More than two levels of nesting are not supported
Nested if-else statements can be used to handle multiple conditions
They must always have an else block

m_Item1.Explanation: Nested if-else statements can be used to handle multiple conditions by placing one if-else statement inside another.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the else block behave if there is no if condition before it?

Options.OptionText: It behaves like a standalone if statement

m_Item1.Explanation: An else block must always follow an if or else if condition; otherwise, it results in a syntax error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Choose the incorrect statement about if-else structures in C.

Options.OptionText: An if statement can exist alone without an else
Every else must follow an if
The else block can have another if structure
An if statement must always include an else

m_Item1.Explanation: An if statement does not need to include an else block, making option D incorrect.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In an if-else-if ladder, which block is executed?

Options.OptionText: All if and else if blocks
Only the first block that is true
Both else and else if blocks

m_Item1.Explanation: In an if-else-if ladder, only the first true condition's block is executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following code?
int x = 10;
if (x < 10)
x += 1;
else
x -= 1;
printf("%d", x);
m_Item1.Explanation: Since x is not less than 10, the else block executes, decrementing x to 9.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be printed by this code segment?
int b = 0;
if (b)
printf("True");
else
printf("False");
m_Item1.Explanation: In C, 0 is considered false; hence the else block executes, printing "False".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What does the following code return?
int val = 1;
if (val = 0)
printf("Zero");
else
printf("One");
m_Item1.Explanation: The assignment in the if condition (val = 0) always evaluates to true (ignoring the actual condition), so else executes, printing "One".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the outcome of this C code block?
int a = 6, b = 5;
if (a < b)
printf("A is less");
else if (a == b)
printf("Equal");
else
printf("B is less");
m_Item1.Explanation: The condition a < b is false and a == b is false, so the else block executes, displaying "B is less".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Determine the output of this code:
int n = 3;
if (n % 2) {
printf("Odd");
} else {
printf("Even");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: n % 2 returns true (non-zero) for odd numbers, executing the true block, hence printing "Odd".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Identify the correct replacement for the following code to avoid syntax errors.
int x = 3;
if x == 3
printf("Three");
m_Item1.Explanation: The correct syntax requires parentheses around the condition, i.e., if (x == 3).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly represents the syntax of an if-else-if ladder in C?

Options.OptionText: if (condition1) { statements; } else if (condition2) { statements; } else { statements; }
if (condition1) else if { statements; } else { statements; }

m_Item1.Explanation: The correct syntax for an if-else-if ladder is to use 'if' followed by 'else if' and a final 'else'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code snippet?
int x = 10;
if(x > 5) {
printf("A");
} else if (x < 8) {
printf("B");
} else {
printf("C");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Since x is 10 which is greater than 5, the first if condition is true, hence 'A' will be printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In an if-else-if ladder, when two conditions are true, which block will be executed?
m_Item1.Explanation: In an if-else-if ladder, once a true condition is found, no further conditions are evaluated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following conditions correctly checks for a number being between 10 and 20 inclusive?
m_Item1.Explanation: Logical operators should be used to check a number within a range, hence option C is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the code snippet:
int y = 5;
if(y == 5) {
printf("Y");
} else if (y != 5) {
printf("N");
} else {
printf("Z");
}
What is the output?
m_Item1.Explanation: Since y equals 5, the first condition is true, thus 'Y' will be printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many else-if conditions can be added to an if-else-if ladder?
m_Item1.Explanation: There can be an unlimited number of else-if conditions in an if-else-if ladder.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can the final 'else' in an if-else-if ladder be omitted?

Options.OptionText: Only if the first if condition is true

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'else' block is optional in an if-else-if ladder.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the code:
int a = 15;
if (a >= 10) {
printf("First");
} else if (a >= 7) {
printf("Second");
} else {
printf("Third");
}
What is printed?
m_Item1.Explanation: Since 'a' is 15 which meets 'a >= 10', the first condition is true, hence 'First' is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is one potential disadvantage of using an if-else-if ladder?

Options.OptionText: Can be less efficient with a large number of conditions

m_Item1.Explanation: If-else-if ladders might lead to inefficient code execution with many conditions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can replace the 'else if' in an if-else-if ladder to achieve the same logic?
m_Item1.Explanation: Nested if statements can achieve the same logic as an else-if ladder.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Find the correct sequence:
int k = 7;
if (k < 5) {
printf("Small");
} else if (k < 10) {
printf("Medium");
} else {
printf("Large");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: 'k' is 7, which meets the condition of 'k < 10' thus 'Medium' will be printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these control structures can be inefficient for long multiple conditional checks?
m_Item1.Explanation: An if-else-if ladder can become inefficient with a long list of conditions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is correct about an if-else-if ladder?

Options.OptionText: Each if condition is checked independently
The else block is executed only if no if or else-if condition is true
Each block of an if-else-if ladder must have an else

m_Item1.Explanation: The else block executes only if no preceding conditions are true.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Is 'else' mandatory for an if-else-if ladder?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'else' block is not mandatory in an if-else-if ladder.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following code?
int a = 10;
if (a > 5) {
    if (a < 15) {
        printf("A");
    }
}
else {
    printf("B");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The outer if condition is true (10 > 5), so the nested if condition is checked and it is also true (10 < 15), thus printing "A".

m_Item1.QuestionText: In nested if conditions, what is essential to ensure correct execution?
m_Item1.Explanation: Proper syntax is essential for nested if conditions to ensure that the blocks of code execute correctly and do not lead to syntax errors.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following code?
int num = 8;
if (num % 4 == 0) {
    if (num % 8 == 0) {
        printf("Divisible by 8");
    } else {
        printf("Divisible by 4 not 8");
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The number 8 is divisible by both 4 and 8, so the first if condition is true and the second if condition is also true, printing "Divisible by 8".

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider a nested if statement with three levels. What happens if the first condition is false?

Options.OptionText: Only the second condition is checked

m_Item1.Explanation: If the first condition in a nested if statement is false, all subsequent nested if conditions are skipped, and their associated code blocks are not executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be displayed when executing the following code?
int x = 1;
if (x == 1) {
    if (x > 0) {
        printf("Positive 1");
    }
}
if (x < 0) {
    printf("Negative 1");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The first if condition is true, so the nested if condition is checked and true, printing "Positive 1"; the second if is false, so "Negative 1" is not printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements regarding nested if statements is TRUE?

Options.OptionText: Nested if statements can only have two levels at most
Nested if conditions can lead to increased code complexity
Nested if statements do not require else blocks
The innermost if condition always executes

m_Item1.Explanation: Using nested if can result in more complex and harder-to-read code if not structured properly.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following code?
int a = 5, b = 10;
if (a < 10) {
    if (b > a) {
        printf("Valid");
    }
} else {
    printf("Invalid");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The outer if condition (5 < 10) is true, and the nested if condition (10 > 5) is also true, resulting in "Valid" being printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a properly nested if structure, when the inner if statement is executed, what can be concluded?

Options.OptionText: The outer if condition was false
The outer if condition was true

m_Item1.Explanation: For the nested if structure to reach and execute the second (nested) if statement, the first if condition must evaluate to true.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code block. What will be the value displayed?
int val = 20;
if (val > 10) {
    if (val < 30) {
        val += 5;
    } else {
        val -= 5;
    }
}
printf("%d", val);
m_Item1.Explanation: The condition (val > 10 && val < 30) is true, so 5 is added to val, making it 25.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a potential disadvantage of heavily using nested if statements?
m_Item1.Explanation: Using nested if does not improve processor speed; it may introduce complexity, leading to reduced readability and harder debugging.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following?
int x = 5, y = 10;
if (x != y) {
    if (y - x == 5) {
        printf("Difference 5");
    }
} else {
    printf("Equal");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The outer if condition (x != y) is true and the nested if condition (y - x == 5) is true, so "Difference 5" is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of a switch-case statement in C?

Options.OptionText: To repeat a block of code multiple times
To allow multiple conditions to be tested
To execute different code blocks based on specific integer or character variable values
To manage memory allocation in the heap

m_Item1.Explanation: A switch-case statement is used to execute different parts of code based on the value of an integer, character, or enumeration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can switch-case statements in C handle ranges of values?

Options.OptionText: Yes, with the use of conditional expressions
Yes, by specifying a range in each case label
No, they can only evaluate single constants
No, they only work with strings

m_Item1.Explanation: Switch-case statements evaluate the expression and jump to the matching case label that corresponds to a constant value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which data types are valid for switch statement expressions in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: Switch statements in C can only evaluate expressions that result in integer or character types.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a break statement is omitted in a case of a switch?

Options.OptionText: The next iteration of the loop begins
The program encounters a compilation error

m_Item1.Explanation: Omitting a break statement leads to fall-through, causing the execution of subsequent case blocks until a break or the end of the switch is reached.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to execute code when none of the case values match in a switch statement?
m_Item1.Explanation: The default keyword is used in a switch statement to execute a block of code if none of the case values match.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Is it mandatory to have a default case in a switch statement?

Options.OptionText: Only if there are more than two cases

m_Item1.Explanation: The default case is optional in a switch statement, although it is good practice to include it for handling unexpected values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the return type of a switch statement?

Options.OptionText: The same as the expression in switch
Switch statements do not have a return type

m_Item1.Explanation: Switch statements do not return any value; they are control flow constructs that direct the execution to appropriate case blocks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a switch-case statement, how are multiple labels for a single block of code specified?

Options.OptionText: Duplicating the code block with different labels

m_Item1.Explanation: Multiple labels for a single block of code can be specified by using multiple case statements before the code block.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if two case labels have the same value?

Options.OptionText: The first case will be executed, and the rest ignored
The program will execute both cases
The program will encounter a compilation error
It will execute the default case instead

m_Item1.Explanation: Duplicate case values in the same switch statement will cause a compilation error because each case must represent a unique label.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a switch statement be effectively used with string variables in standard C?

Options.OptionText: No, only integer and character types

m_Item1.Explanation: In standard C, switch statements are designed to work only with integer or character variables; other types require workarounds.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following options correctly demonstrates a switch statement syntax in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: The standard switch statement syntax in C includes the switch keyword, followed by an expression in parentheses and a series of case labels.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenario would you use a switch-case statement over if-else if?

Options.OptionText: When needing to evaluate complex conditions
When testing a single expression against multiple constant values

m_Item1.Explanation: Switch-case statements are best suited for cases where a single variable or expression is compared against multiple constant values efficiently.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which part of the switch statement allows for cleaning up after every case statement?
m_Item1.Explanation: The break keyword is used to terminate a case in a switch statement and exit the switch block.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output if no case matches and no default is provided?

Options.OptionText: The program exits with an error

m_Item1.Explanation: If no case matches and no default is provided, the control simply exits the switch statement without executing any part within.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What type of error occurs if you forget to include a break after each case?
m_Item1.Explanation: Omitting a break typically causes a logical error due to unintended fall-through behavior within the switch statement.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can a switch statement be structured to handle an error condition?
m_Item1.Explanation: Including a default case can allow the switch statement to handle unexpected values or error conditions effectively.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following operations is not possible with a standard switch statement?
m_Item1.Explanation: Standard C switch statements cannot handle strings directly; they are limited to integer or character values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the ternary operator also known as in C programming?
m_Item1.Explanation: The ternary operator is known as both 'Question mark colon operator' and 'Conditional expression'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is the ternary operator represented in C programming?
m_Item1.Explanation: The ternary operator is represented by the symbols ?: in C programming.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following snippets demonstrates the correct use of the ternary operator?
m_Item1.Explanation: The ternary operator is used in the format: condition ? expression1 : expression2;

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the output of the following code? int a = 5, b = 3; printf("%d", a > b ? a : b);
m_Item1.Explanation: The condition (a > b) is true, so the output is the value of 'a', which is 5.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can replace an if-else statement?
m_Item1.Explanation: The ternary operator is a shorthand for the if-else statement for simple conditional expressions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If the expression using a ternary operator is 'x > y ? x : y', what does it imply?

Options.OptionText: Always assigns y to the result
Assigns the greater value between x and y to the result
Always assigns x to the result
Assigns the smaller value between x and y to result

m_Item1.Explanation: The ternary operator assigns x to the result if x is greater than y, otherwise y is assigned.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a drawback of using the ternary operator excessively in C?
m_Item1.Explanation: Excessive use of the ternary operator can make the code less readable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Using the ternary operator, how can you assign a value conditionally to a variable 'z' where 'z' is 10 if x equals y, otherwise 20?
m_Item1.Explanation: The expression uses the ternary operator correctly to check if x equals y and assigns the corresponding value.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which data types can the operands of the ternary operator be?
m_Item1.Explanation: Operands of the ternary operator can be of any data type since it evaluates expressions based on conditions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if the ternary operator is used without an else condition?
m_Item1.Explanation: The ternary operator requires both true and false expressions, thus omitting any part will cause a compilation error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When is the expression after the colon in a ternary operator executed?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a ternary operation, the expression after the colon is executed when the condition evaluates to false.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
    {
     char c='a';
     lable: switch  (c)
          {
           case 'a': ++c;  
                 goto lable;

           case 'b': printf("%c", c++);

           case 'c':  printf("%c", c);
          }
     }
m_Item1.Explanation: In this case when Case 'a' get executed ++c means C get increased and Swich case for b is executed and as there is post increament of c the value of c is  printed as b and then get changed to c, and as there is no break statement case c also get executed. hence answer will be bc.
